method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano is a mostly submarine volcano on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, where the Pacific Plate is subducting beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] It rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extends roughly 20 km across, with only small parts of its summit area emerging above sea level as Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Its eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

The two original islands were interpreted as the exposed northern and western remnants of the rim of a large submarine caldera.[2] Early geological descriptions noted that Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were elongated and tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga contained alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash that dipped gently away from the inferred caldera centre.[2]

High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys later confirmed a submarine depression at the inferred caldera site, measuring about 4 km by 2 km and 150 m deep.[2] Other descriptions characterize the summit caldera as roughly 5 km in diameter.[4] The caldera morphology is interpreted as the product of explosive collapse during an earlier eruption of the volcanic edifice.[2]

The 2014–2015 eruption built a large tephra cone that connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, creating the single island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4][5] Before the 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m.[5] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5] That eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have reached volcanic explosivity index 5, although further analysis was needed to refine the erupted volume estimate.[5]

==Geography==
The former island lay in the central part of the Tongan archipelago, about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou and about 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island.[4] It was uninhabited, and its nearest major population centre was Tongatapu, where about 75% of Tonga’s population resides.[4] The site is in the South Pacific on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec island arc, a tectonically active zone produced by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5] 

Before its destruction in 2022, the landform consisted of two older islands linked by a younger tephra deposit formed during the 2014–2015 eruption.[1][4] Because the island was newly formed and rapidly changing, satellite observations of its erosion and geomorphic evolution were used as an analogue for interpreting similar landforms on Mars.[5] After the January 2022 eruption, ash was deposited across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua island groups, with reported thicknesses of about 20–30 mm on Tongatapu.[4] The same eruption generated tsunami waves that affected inhabited islands throughout Tonga and propagated across the Pacific to coastlines in South America, North America and Northeast Asia.[4]

==Eruptions==
On 15 January 2022 at about 5 p.m. local time, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently, producing massive explosions and lofting gas and ash about 36 miles into the atmosphere.[4] Satellite-era analyses measured a plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, and estimated the initial explosion’s energy release at 10–28 exajoules, probably greater than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[5] The eruptive vent was about 200 m below sea level, allowing seawater to interact explosively with very hot magma.[1][3] Researchers suggested that weakening or collapse of part of the caldera rim may have allowed unusually large volumes of water to enter the magma system, making the event far more intense than a typical Surtseyan eruption.[3]

The eruption generated tsunamis that struck Tonga, where run-up reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people, and causing four deaths.[4] Waves also affected coastlines across the Pacific, contributing to two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and causing damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Because no seismometers were installed on or very near the volcano and the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles away, scientists had limited local seismic data for reconstructing the eruption sequence.[4] The USGS National Earthquake Information Center and Volcano Disaster Assistance Program subsequently developed methods to identify nearby earthquakes that automated systems had missed and compiled a near-real-time earthquake catalogue for the period after the eruption.[4]

Marine surveys after the eruption found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, as well as gouged valleys and large sediment deposits on the seafloor.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the event and was later found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4] Scientists recorded up to about 1.67 cubic miles of displaced material, with the total likely higher because not all deposits had been mapped.[4]

The eruption injected large quantities of volcanic aerosols and water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing total stratospheric water content by about 10%.[2] It also released sulfur dioxide that was converted into sulfate aerosols, which reflect sunlight and tend to cool the atmosphere, while stratospheric water vapour can have a warming influence.[1] Subsequent climate studies found that the eruption produced a net slight cooling effect from 2022, including about 0.1 °C of cooling over the Southern Hemisphere, although some observations indicated a very small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere.[1]

==Footnotes==
* Scientific literature abbreviates Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai as HTHH and locates the underwater volcano at 20.536°S, 175.382°W, about 150 m below sea level in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc.[1]
* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated VEI of about 6.[1]
* Reported effects of the eruption included a plume reaching up to 57 km in altitude, tephra spread over an area at least 600 km in diameter, and the release of about 2.9 × 10^15 g of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material.[1]
* Some commentary identified a submarine landslide as a likely trigger for the explosive phase, noting that emerging island volcanoes can be unstable and prone to landsliding.[2]
* The eruption generated tsunami waves affecting Tonga and was also associated with tsunami observations across the Pacific, including Australia, Japan, and the west coasts of North and South America.[2]
* Reviews of the event emphasized its significance for understanding historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis, as well as the need for field studies of tsunami deposits and improved modelling of atmosphere–ocean coupling.[3]
* Climate studies reported that the eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapour into the stratosphere, producing cooling in the mid-stratosphere for more than a year while warming parts of the lower stratosphere.[4]
* A later UCLA-led analysis reported that the eruption produced net cooling rather than warming over the Southern Hemisphere, estimating a temperature reduction of about 0.1 °C by the end of 2022 and 2023.[5]
* Data from one 2024 study on the eruption were made available through the PANGAEA database and Figshare, with supplementary material provided online by the journal.[1]

* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated VEI of about 6.[1]
